original_abstract: The selective modification of nitrogen heteroaromatics enables the development of new chemical tools and accelerates drug discovery. While methods that focus on expanding or contracting the skeletal structures of heteroaromatics are emerging, methods for the direct exchange of single core atoms remain limited. Here, we present a method for <sup>14</sup>N → <sup>15</sup>N isotopic exchange for several aromatic nitrogen heterocycles. This nitrogen isotope transmutation occurs through activation of the heteroaromatic substrate by triflylation of a nitrogen atom, followed by a ring-opening/ring-closure sequence mediated by <sup>15</sup>N-aspartate to effect the isotopic exchange of the nitrogen atom. Key to the success of this transformation is the formation of an isolable <sup>15</sup>N-succinyl intermediate, which undergoes elimination to give the isotopically labeled heterocycle. These transformations occur under mild conditions in high chemical and isotopic yields.
humanized_abstract: Selective modification of nitrogen heteroaromatics can lead to the discovery of new tools and increase the pace of drug discovery. While methods that increase or decrease the size of the heteroaromatic ring are being developed, the direct exchange of single core atoms is relatively rare. Here, we describe the <sup>14</sup>N → <sup>15</sup>N isotopic exchange of several aromatic nitrogen heterocycles. The <sup>14</sup>N → <sup>15</sup>N nitrogen isotope transmutation involves triflylation of the heteroaromatic substrate, followed by a ring-opening/ring-closure sequence mediated by <sup>15</sup>N-aspartate to facilitate the <sup>14</sup>N → <sup>15</sup>N exchange of the nitrogen atom of the heterocycle. The formation of the isolable <sup>15</sup>N-succinyl intermediate is crucial to this transformation as it allows the intermediate to undergo an elimination reaction to afford the <sup>15</sup>N isotopologues of the heterocycles in high yields and with excellent isotopic incorporation.

The <sup>14</sup>N → <sup>15</sup>N nitrogen isotope transmutation involves triflylation of the heteroaromatic substrate, followed by a ring-opening/ring-closure sequence mediated by <sup>15</sup>N-aspartate to facilitate the <sup>14</sup>N → <sup>15</sup>N exchange of the nitrogen atom of the heterocycle. The formation of the isolable <sup>15</sup>N-succinyl intermediate is crucial to this transformation as it allows the intermediate to undergo an elimination reaction to afford the <sup>15</sup>N isotopologues of the heterocycles in high yields and with excellent isotopic incorporation.
